speaker: interviewer
text: And any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got to Hoof, and I was completely bewildered because nothing looked familiar at all. I mean, it's asking a bit much after 50 years-- oh, more than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait till you get to my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Start from scratch?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Hoof, and I'm very puzzled because nothing looks familiar. And we found what is supposedly the city hall, which later turns out to be my old school. And my friend speaks to the lady in charge. She said, I'm sorry, listen, I can't help you. But there is-- I know someone who can.

speaker: interviewer
text: It appears three retired people have set themselves up as-- what's the word? They're looking into the past of the Jews of Hessen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Historians for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Historians, if you like. So we went to this gentleman's house. And he has everything on microfilm. And of course, I can't speak to him properly because I have no German. And he, first of all, wants to know do I want my fath-- my grandfather's history? I mean, he goes back to 1700 and something-- has this all on the computer and gives me a copy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we talk about my family. And he said, oh, yes, I know it-- I know that well. And we ask him, how? Well, he said, amongst all the deportees, the people who were transported in the cattle trucks from Kassel, were two boys who have Russian names. They were sent to a prison-- to a camp. And when the war was over, they were released by Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came back to Kassel-- the only people who came back to Kassel-- and swore affidavits to what they had seen. And one of these affidavits was about my family, because there is no way you could miss one woman with six small children. The eldest was 11 and the youngest was two. They arrived at Riga. And this has been sworn as an affidavit.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941-- December 1941. And the Nazis, or whoever was in charge, wanted to separate my mother from my brothers and sisters. And my 11-year-old brother stood in front of my family with his arms stretched out, as if to embrace them, and said-- turned around to the Nazis and said, we are going to die. We will die together. And they shot them there and then. All my life I've imagined them in gas chambers. I've watched all these dreadful television programs, all these films, in the hope of seeing one. And now I discover they died together. All my life I have lived with guilt. My guilt has been that I am alive and they are dead. The guilt has become no less. But the relief of knowing what actually happened-- and very few of us survivors actually know what happened to their family. It makes a great difference. They died a clean death. While we were talking to-- Herr Schunder his name was-- his wife had phoned up the Kassel newspapers. And they suddenly arrived at the doorstep, wanting to interview me. And it appears that the Nazis wanted to smash all the gravestones, so there wasn't a Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these gravestones that were left were taken by the church and intermingled with their own. And after the war, in a clearing in the forest, they had set up all these gravestones. There are no bodies there, no graves, but the gravestones. And Herr Schunder knew this one particular one, which was my great-great grandmother. And the photographers came from the newspaper, and the reporters came from the newspaper, and they were asking me questions, taking photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in a dreadful state. Physically, mentally, I was completely exhausted. And they insisted on asking me questions in German. And I had to reply in German, which is all but impossible to me. I only learned it as a child. And I spoke as a child. And when we eventually finished, the reporter turned round to me and spoke in the most beautiful English, asking me where I actually lived. And he actually had been in holiday where I live. But it was such a relief to know what happened, as I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a closure.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- I could-- I could mourn them, knowing they died together. I have gravestone. I don't know-- I wouldn't go to Riga if someone paid me. And that journey to Germany was the first and last. It was one of the most painful things I have ever done. It hurt so badly. It's reliving my past. Herr Schunder insisted on taking me to that big house, where we were hidden in. And I spoke to the-- to the daughter. And she had no English. And we walked around. And I suddenly said-- you couldn't see around the corner of this enormous house. I said, there are a row of-- in German-- a row of apple trees. I'm going back 50 years-- or rather 40, 40-odd. And she took one hand, and her husband took the other, and they walked me round the house. And there were the apple trees. We walked a little further and I said, there's a tree missing. This is at the edge of a forest amongst hundreds and hundreds of trees. I said, there's a tree missing. There was a walnut tree here. And the husband looked at the wife. They did-- their mouths were open. And she said, well, that hasn't been here for 30 years. And this is how I went through the village, identifying my grandparents' houses, my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your house was still standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they've--

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: They've completely demolished it and built it in the modern style-- the old modern style. But never again-- I couldn't go through that again. I wouldn't want to go through that again. Once is quite enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there, you've been involved in the Anne Frank exhibit and different things-- teaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I lecture once a month on a Jewish childhood in Germany, because people knew about this. I've had articles about me in the local paper. I was contacted last August by the displays manager of a big center of literature in Swansea. And he said, could I help him, because the Anne Frank exhibition was coming to Swansea. And I said, yes. Of all the things I wanted, I want to get to the children. Because he said they were going to bus in children. I said, I speak to adults, but all I want is to get to schools. So I said, yeah, nothing I want more. So I had to go on radio, on television, in the local papers to advertise the exhibition. And we had a meeting with the-- what was left of the Jewish congregation, which is dreadfully small. And I had some offers of help, because there's no way you can do this on your own. I mean, there's 70 screens with pictures on them. And then there's a 30-minute video. So you need a lot of help. Anyway, we worked it out. And then we had the preview. I had never seen the exhibition. I'd read the book, never seen the exhibition. And I didn't know what to expect. I'd seen the video. And the night of the preview, pre-opening, there was a big reception and I was asked to speak. And I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first time in my life I got a standing ovation, which shook me rigid. And next day, I started taking children round. And this went on four days a week for a month. And eventually, we had 6,000 children. And just going round the exhibition took an hour. So I had, as I said, lots of help for other guides. But there were schools that asked for me. There were people who came up to me-- are you a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children were from 10 to 18. And I found such joy. Because the exhibition wasn't an Anne Frank exhibition. It was the rise of National Socialism, the rise of Hitler, all my lifetime, and then coming back to what is yet to come. And I have to explain to these children, you can't-- this is not my story. This is the Anne Frank story. So you can't put yourself-- so you can't just tell them your story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's certain parts where you can say, you know, Hitler supposed to-- there's one photograph, Hitler with a child. Hitler loves children. And I turn around to the groups I have, and I said, out of six million, I can't tell you how many of those were children. But I can tell you how many out of one family died. And I said, that's my family. Out of seven children, I said, six died-- and the look on those faces.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you come to Kristallnacht, when I explain I was-- we were actually in the synagogue that they were burning. The fact that the dates didn't match was neither here nor there. And the impression on these children-- I mean, I was absolutely exhausted. I was ill right through it. But I have never done anything that I consider so worthwhile. I've got teachers who are asking me when I can speak to the schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the thing that came out of this, which is so important to me-- I've spent my life talking to God saying, why am I alive and the others not? And only to the end of this exhibition did I realize I'm here as a teacher, to teach these children what not to do in the future, to tell them about the past so the future will not be a repetition of the past.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's been so important, that I have been able to get through to children from 10 to 18. I've had letters of thanks from schools to say how much this means. But to get through from 10- to 18-year-olds, you have to adjust each time. You can't talk down to children. You have to put yourself in their place. But the results have been so wonderful, absolutely wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a specific message that you want to take the opportunity, while you're speaking to the future generations, on this video? Do you want--

speaker: interviewee
text: I've a-- my message you've already heard. I will not sit still and let the past repeat itself-- not for my grandchildren, not for anybody's grandchildren. I will do all I can, for whatever life God gives me, for what ever years I've got left, to teach as many children as I can. Because I now have the opportunity to get to children, something I've wanted to do for years. I've only been able to talk about this for 10 years. But now I can get to the children, because I am the past. They have a future. And you must teach the future. You must teach about-- you must teach the future about the past, so that the past must never repeat itself. Other people must not go through what I've gone through and what hundreds and thousands like me have gone through. Because everybody I know in the kindertransport-- they've all got a different story. But I feel as if I have been chosen. Because of the exhibition, I've got the chance to do what I can. I've always done voluntary work in Swansea, from cooking for 30-odd elderly people-- and I'm 67, which is ridiculous-- to doing all sorts of things. I belong to something called Childline, which raises money for an organization that just listens. A child can phone in. And a child will tell you more on the phone than he can facing you. And that's another part of the Anne Frank exhibition. They've read the book. Some of them have read the book. But a child hasn't the imagination to see what's in a book. But show them pictures, like the exhibition-- they see in pictures what they've read and it makes sense. Because no child, even 18-year-old, can imagine what we went through. But show them a picture of it. Show them a picture of the people going into the camp in their pajamas, in their striped uniform. That makes sense. That's the only way you can get through. And then you know you have to-- you have to put your heart and soul into this. You can't do it half-heartedly. And I'm the only one who could say this is no story. I was there. And this is what I kept saying. I was there. I lived through this. And of course, afterwards, they wanted to know how I lived and why my family died. So it's a continuation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any special pictures or documents you would like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have. And I would like to share.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There's [? Oma, ?] with her snow-white hair. And as I said, she's only a little thing-- and Opa looking very stern. And behind--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandparents, I'm sorry. And behind my grandmother is my mother in her teens. On the far side is the aunt who married my father's brother. So those, except for two of them went-- three of them-- they all went to the Argentine.

speaker: interviewer
text: With--

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is a very precious photograph because I didn't get that until a few years ago, when my family here in Cincinnati had it copied. But I didn't have any of these at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this taken in Hoof by Kassel?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Hoof, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hoof.

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably in the garden of my grandparents' house. But it's so precious.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please explain this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the thing nearest my heart. It's the only picture of my family-- my mother with Ludwig, my father holding my sister Ruth. Then there's Rolf, my brother. And then there's Heinz. And the one with girly-looking hair is Sally, known as [? Bubbe. ?] And on the far side is me, looking very grown up. But this-- this is the only photograph I have of my family, as they were then. It's a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the year this was taken? Do you have an idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say it's about '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: '37? Was this by the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is still in the old house, just before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Literally, it must have been a week or so before. I don't remember it being taken. But I-- it couldn't have been any earlier-- any later than that. But I'm standing on one step lower to make myself look bigger. But it's the most precious of my possessions. That's me, taken in the orphanage, when they said, smile. You can see the effort of the smile. This is the photograph that was sent to Wales. And this is the one I was chosen from. And this is why I am still alive, just from a little photograph like that. Someone thought I would be useful. And I have been, I hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain this to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only document that would let you out of Germany, as you can see the eagles on it. And that's dated 3rd of June '39. And without that, I couldn't have come. But I only managed to find-- or someone found it for me-- two years ago. And I've been without a document to say I'm alive all those years. But that does. That proves it. Thank you. This is the document that allowed me to come into Great Britain. And without that, I wouldn't be here either. And again, I only had that two years ago. And this is a document issued to my brother Rolf in Germany. I don't know if you can see it, but every Jewish male had Israel tacked on to his first name. It isn't his name. His name is Rolf. But Israel is on every male passport from Germany. And I managed to find this not very long ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: It came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that through that book, when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. I'll tell you with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right you were going to get to that. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the same document, but for my brother Heinz. Again, you can see the Israel and his thumb print or fingerprint. And these were taken not long before they were deported. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a page from the Kassel memorial book, which tells of my family, the only family in the book with seven people who died. This is Johanna Wertheim, my mother. And this is Rolf, my brother next to me. This is Heinz, again, in 1931. And then we have Sally, who was known as Bubbe. And he was born in '33.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we have Ruth, my sister, born in 1935 and Ludwig, who I only knew as a baby, born in '37, and Zilla, who I never saw, born in '39. The documents you said you saw earlier-- amongst that page was a photograph of my brother Rolf. And a friend wrote to Kassel saying, if they have a photograph of my brother Rolf, why don't they have a photograph of the others? And Kassel sent me the document you have seen, with the photographs of Rolf and Heinz, and also the original photographs. And that's how I managed to get those documents only some 10 years ago. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are photographs of the two boys who returned to Kassel as men from the camps and swore the affidavits of what they saw and how my mother died. So they're very important people to me. They too have died since. One, unfortunately, died in a mental home from all his suffering. But they have done a great thing for me, because they swore to what they had seen, and I know that's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: They bore witness to the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: They bore witness to the truth. And there are not many who could say that. Thank you. That's a photograph taken when I was in Hoof four years ago. And it was taken by the gravestone of my great-great grandmother. And again, it's very important. I was very tired and I didn't want my photograph taken. But there's nothing on Earth that I wouldn't have done so that I could get that photograph of just one gravestone. These-- these are photograph-- a photograph taken under pressure, because none of us like having photographs taken-- only a month or so ago of myself, my daughter Anita, and my son Martin, of who I'm very proud. This is a photograph of my daughter Anita, who is now 47, my son-in-law Hugh, my grandson Daniel, who is 18, and my granddaughter Hannah, after my mother, who is now 13. My daughter and her husband are both teachers and very, very good teachers, so I'm told-- and again one of my proudest and most joyous photographs. I had to steal this one. They wouldn't give it to me. [LAUGHS] As I said, we all hate having our photographs taken. But my grandchildren, to please me-- well, I wanted a photograph of a very, very proud and happy grandmother. So they had this to say to please me-- literally to please me. My grandson absolutely hates and runs away from photographers. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my son Martin, and he's 41, and his wife Wendy-- a delightful couple. How can a mother not say anything else? But they are. All my family, thank God-- are all very happy together. And I ask for nothing more than that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me getting older by the minute and my husband of 24 years dancing, or trying. He has two left feet. But this was our daughter's 21st wedding anniversary. And it was our joy to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- that's Colin.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Colin, yes. None of us are young-- getting younger. But we'll grow old together. I think we've got there already. [LAUGHS] Thank you.
